narrative:
First Warn Forecast: Thursday, May 14, 2026

A few thunderstorms are possible late tonight into early Friday, especially north of I-70.
Isolated strong storms tonight may produce small hail and gusty winds.
Severe weather chances gradually increase Friday into Saturday across the Plains and Mid-Mississippi Valley.
Sunday through Tuesday may feature a much more active and potentially dangerous severe weather pattern across portions of the Plains and Midwest.
Missouri and Illinois residents should closely monitor forecasts through early next week.

A quiet and comfortable start to Thursday will give way to a much more active weather pattern over the next several days. High pressure sliding east of the region today will allow southerly winds to return across Missouri and Illinois. Sunshine and warmer temperatures will dominate through the afternoon, with highs reaching the upper 70s to near 80 degrees.

By tonight, moisture will begin rapidly increasing as a strengthening low-level jet develops across the Plains and Mid-Mississippi Valley. This will help spark scattered elevated thunderstorms across portions of northern Missouri, southern Iowa, and western Illinois late tonight into early Friday morning. While the overall severe threat tonight remains relatively low locally, the atmosphere will contain steep lapse rates and increasing elevated instability capable of supporting a few stronger storms.

The primary concerns overnight into Friday morning will be isolated hail and localized strong wind gusts, especially with any storm that can briefly intensify while moving southeastward. Much of the more significant severe weather threat today will remain west of the Mississippi River, particularly across central and eastern Kansas where the Storm Prediction Center has outlined a Slight Risk for severe thunderstorms.

Friday begins a gradual transition into a more unstable and humid pattern across the central United States. Temperatures will climb well into the 80s while dew points surge into the mid and upper 60s. That combination alone creates plenty of atmospheric fuel for thunderstorm development.

By Friday afternoon and evening, severe weather chances will increase across parts of Kansas, Nebraska, and western Missouri as a surface trough develops and instability rapidly builds. Forecast soundings across portions of northeast Kansas indicate extreme instability values approaching 3500 to 4000 J/kg of CAPE along with steep mid-level lapse rates and sufficient wind shear for organized supercells.

While the highest severe weather risk Friday remains west of the St. Louis region, isolated severe storms could still drift eastward into portions of Missouri and western Illinois Friday night. The primary threats with any stronger storms would be large hail and damaging winds.

Saturday is becoming increasingly concerning across the central Plains into portions of the lower and mid-Missouri Valley. A strengthening upper-level trough moving into the western United States will help increase southwest flow aloft across the Plains while rich Gulf moisture continues streaming northward.

This setup could support intense supercell thunderstorms capable of producing very large hail, damaging winds, and tornadoes across parts of Kansas, Nebraska, and potentially northwest Missouri Saturday afternoon and evening. Some forecast guidance even suggests hail larger than 3 inches in diameter and the possibility of a strong tornado where low-level wind shear becomes maximized.

Locally, Saturday across eastern Missouri and southwest Illinois currently appears more conditional. While scattered thunderstorms remain possible, the greatest organized severe weather threat may remain just west of the area. Still, any subtle eastward shift in the setup could bring a stronger severe threat closer to the Mississippi River.

Sunday into Monday bears especially close watching.

The Storm Prediction Center is already discussing the potential for a broader severe weather outbreak somewhere across the Plains into portions of the Midwest during this time frame. A large upper trough ejecting eastward combined with strengthening low-level jet dynamics, increasing deep-layer shear, and a very unstable air mass may create a volatile severe weather environment.

Sunday afternoon and evening could feature widespread severe thunderstorms from the central Plains into the Mid-Mississippi Valley. Supercells capable of tornadoes, large hail, and damaging winds appear possible. Exactly where the highest-end threat develops remains uncertain this far out, but confidence is increasing that at least part of the region will experience a significant severe weather episode.

By Monday, forecast models show a negatively tilted trough ejecting into the Plains with a strengthening dryline and cold front setup. This pattern is often associated with organized severe weather events in May. If current trends continue, Monday afternoon and evening could support another round of dangerous thunderstorms capable of tornadoes, very large hail, and widespread damaging winds somewhere across the central United States extending toward the Mid-Mississippi Valley.

Tuesday may ultimately become the day with the more focused local threat for eastern Missouri and western Illinois as the cold front slowly advances eastward into the region. Very warm temperatures, high humidity, and strong instability ahead of the boundary could support additional severe thunderstorm development. Depending on how storms evolve Sunday and Monday, Tuesday could feature another round of organized severe weather locally with all hazards possible, including damaging winds, large hail, isolated tornadoes, and torrential rainfall.

The exact placement and timing of the highest severe threat zones will continue evolving over the coming days, but confidence is growing that the central United States will enter a much more active severe weather pattern through early next week.

Now is the time to review severe weather safety plans, ensure weather alerts are enabled on your devices, and stay updated with forecast changes as we move deeper into this potentially volatile stretch of spring weather.
